perturbation.original_text: Unless a shorter period is indicated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not insert any claims of breach of this Agreement or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information made more than five <PAGE> 2 (5) years from the date of the CITR under which such information was disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless a shorter period is indicated in the applicable CITR, the disclosing party will not insert any claims of breach of this Agreement or misappropriation of trade secrets against the receiving party arising from the receiving party's disclosure of the disclosing party's Confidential Information made more than one (1) year from the date of the CITR under which such information was disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shortens the period for asserting claims of breach or misappropriation to one year, which may conflict with the statute of limitations for trade secret misappropriation under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) and similar state laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. While the DTSA itself does not specify a statute of limitations, most states have statutes of limitations for trade secret misappropriation claims that are longer than one year. For example, California's statute of limitations is 3 years. By limiting the claim period to one year, the contract potentially restricts the disclosing party's ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation within the timeframe allowed by applicable law.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the claim period to one year, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations for trade secret misappropriation, which are generally longer than one year. The original text provided a 5 year window, which is now reduced. The snippet provided, 18 USC 1836, allows for civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation, implying a time frame in which such proceedings can occur. By reducing the window to one year, the contract restricts the disclosing party's ability to pursue legal action, which is provided for in 18 USC 1836.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by laws of the State of Delaware without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by laws of the State of California without reference to conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: The contract specifies Delaware law, but the modification changes the governing law to California. If the contract is performed primarily in California and involves California employees, certain California labor laws may apply regardless of the chosen governing law. The modified text now applies CA law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 925 restricts employers from requiring employees who primarily reside and work in California to adjudicate disputes outside of California or to be governed by the law of another state. The change in the modified text applies CA law, but it could still be problematic if it includes clauses that violate California labor laws.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 3. PRIVILEGES AND IMMUNITIES [920 - 1139] ( Part 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts Against Public Policy [920 - 927] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 925. (a) An employer shall not require an employee who primarily resides and works in California, as a condition of employment, to agree to a provision that would do either of the following: (1) Require the employee to adjudicate outside of California a claim arising in California. (2) Deprive the employee of the substantive protection of California law with respect to a controversy arising in California. (b) Any provision of a contract that violates subdivision (a) is voidable by the employee, and if a provision is rendered void at the request of the employee, the matter shall be adjudicated in California and California law shall govern the dispute. (c) In addition to injunctive relief and any other remedies available, a court may award an employee who is enforcing his or her rights under this section reasonable attorney’s fees. (d) For purposes of this section, adjudication includes litigation and arbitration. (e) This section shall not apply to a contract with
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from Delaware to California. California Labor Code § 925(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, states that an employer cannot require an employee who primarily resides and works in California to agree to a provision that would deprive them of the substantive protection of California law with respect to a controversy arising in California. By changing the governing law to California, but still potentially including clauses that violate California labor laws as stated in the Law Explanation, the modified text creates a contradiction. The original text specified Delaware law, potentially avoiding CA labor law issues for California-based employees; the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon written notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon verbal notice to the other party: provided that each party's obligations with respect to Confidential Information disclosed during the term of this Agreement will survive any such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring verbal notice for termination contradicts standard contract law principles that generally require written notice for termination to ensure a clear record and prevent disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: While a non-disclosure agreement is not explicitly mentioned in California's Statute of Frauds, requiring verbal termination notice can lead to disputes about whether proper notice was given. The Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. By allowing verbal notice, it creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability issues, especially if there's a disagreement about whether the termination occurred. Even though NDAs are generally enforceable even if not in writing (as they're generally performable within a year), requiring verbal notice for termination introduces legal uncertainty and contradicts the prudence expected in business transactions.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing verbal notice for termination, contradicts the Statute of Frauds' underlying principle of preventing fraud and injury, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2. While the snippets don't explicitly mandate written termination for all contracts, Snippet 2 emphasizes the need for written contracts in specific scenarios and to generally prevent fraud. The modification from written to verbal notice creates ambiguity and potential disputes regarding whether proper termination occurred, thereby increasing the risk of fraud and undermining the legal prudence expected in business transactions, as argued in the Law Explanation. The original text required written notice, ensuring a clear record, a standard undermined by the change.
